title: Pipe lagging and preventing frozen pipes
description: How to lag pipes, what it saves at 2026 prices, and how to stop pipes freezing and bursting. UK guide with costs, pipe sizes and a cold snap checklist.
blurb: Foam lagging costs about £1.50 a metre, cuts heat loss and helps prevent burst pipe claims averaging £17,000.
lede: Pipe lagging is the least glamorous job in home energy saving, and one of the very few that pays for itself twice. The first payback is steady and small: every metre of bare hot pipe running through a cold loft or garage leaks heat you paid for, all season long. The second payback is rare but enormous: a lagged pipe is far less likely to freeze, and a frozen pipe that splits can flood a house. The Association of British Insurers reported around 12,000 burst pipe claims after the cold snaps of 2023, with the average claim coming in above £17,000. Against that, a 2 metre length of foam tube from a DIY shed costs about £3 and takes thirty seconds to fit.
short_answer: Lag every hot water pipe you can reach, and every pipe of any kind in an unheated space such as a loft, garage or under a suspended floor. Foam tube lagging costs roughly £1 to £1.50 per metre and a typical house needs £15 to £30 of it. On gas, 10 metres of bare hot pipe in a cold loft wastes around £29 a year at July 2026 cap rates, so the lagging pays for itself within the first winter, and the freeze protection is worth far more than the energy saving.

sections.h2: What pipe lagging is and which pipes to do first
sections.html:
Pipe lagging is a sleeve of insulation fitted around a pipe. The standard DIY product is a grey polyethylene foam tube, pre-slit down one side so you can push it over an installed pipe without disconnecting anything. You will also see rubber (nitrile) tube, which is denser and better for outdoor runs, and felt or foil wrap for awkward fittings.

Not all pipes matter equally. Work through them in this order:

  • Pipes in unheated spaces. Loft, garage, cellar, outbuildings and the void under a suspended timber floor. These are the freeze risks and the biggest heat losers. Do every pipe here, hot and cold.
  • The primary pipework near the boiler and hot water cylinder. These pipes run hot for hours a day. The first metre or so from the cylinder is required to be insulated under building regulations for good reason. This pairs naturally with a cylinder jacket on an older hot water tank.
  • Long hot water runs to taps. Lagging these will not save a fortune, but it means the slug of hot water left in the pipe after you run a tap stays warm longer, so you waste less water waiting for the next draw.
  • Outside taps and any exposed external pipe. Use an insulated tap cover (about £3 to £5) and weatherproof lagging, or better still, isolate and drain the outside tap before winter.

One pipe deserves special mention: the white plastic condensate pipe from a condensing boiler. If it runs outside to a drain, it is the single most common freeze failure in British homes. When it blocks with ice the boiler shuts down, usually on the coldest morning of the year. Lag it with weatherproof insulation, and if it has long shallow outdoor runs, ask a heating engineer about rerouting or fitting trace heating.

sections.h2: What bare pipes actually cost you
sections.html:
A bare 22mm copper pipe carrying water at around 60°C through a 10°C space loses roughly 35 watts per metre. Good foam lagging with a 25mm wall cuts that to under 10 watts, saving about 25 watts for every metre covered. That sounds trivial until you multiply it across a heating season.

The table below assumes the pipe runs hot for six hours a day across a 240 day heating season, which is typical for primary heating pipework. Gas figures allow for a 90% efficient boiler.

Bare pipe in a cold spaceHeat lost per yearCost on gasCost on electric heating
1 metre36 kWh£2.93£9.40
5 metres180 kWh£14.66£47.00
10 metres360 kWh£29.32£94.00

Assumes a 22mm pipe at 60°C in a 10°C space, hot six hours a day for 240 days, with a 90% efficient gas boiler. Gas at 7.33p and electricity at 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026.

Foam tube costs about £2 to £3 per 2 metre length, so insulating that 10 metre run costs £10 to £15 in materials and pays for itself within the first winter on gas, and within a couple of months on electric heating. Few measures in the whole of home energy saving beat that payback. Even topping up loft insulation, the classic quick win, takes two to three years to repay its cost.

There is a subtlety with pipes under loft insulation: once you insulate a loft floor properly, the loft above it gets colder, because heat from the rooms below no longer warms it. Any pipes and tanks up there are then at greater freeze risk than before. Always lag loft pipework when you insulate the loft floor, and run the insulation under the pipes, never over hot pipes in a way that traps them against the cold side.

sections.h2: Why frozen pipes cost so much more than wasted heat
sections.html:
Water expands by about 9% when it freezes. In a sealed pipe that expansion has nowhere to go, so the pressure splits the pipe wall or pushes a joint apart. The cruel part is that nothing leaks while the ice is in place. The flood starts during the thaw, often while everyone is out at work or away for Christmas, and mains pressure water can release hundreds of litres an hour into ceilings, walls and floors.

The insurance numbers tell the story. The ABI reports that insurers pay out around £1.8 million every day for escape of water damage, and after the freezing spells of 2023 the average burst pipe claim came to more than £17,000. Even with insurance, you face the excess, weeks or months of drying out and repairs, possibly moving out, and higher premiums afterwards. Tenants and people in flats carry extra risk, because one burst pipe can damage several homes below.

Frost needs three things to burst a pipe: water in the pipe, temperatures below freezing for long enough, and no protection. You can attack all three:

  • Remove the water from pipes that do not need it in winter: drain outside taps, garden irrigation and pipework to unheated outbuildings.
  • Keep the temperature up where pipes run: a small amount of background heat protects pipework far more cheaply than a repair.
  • Add protection with lagging on everything exposed. Lagging does not make a pipe freeze-proof, it buys time, and in a typical British cold snap of a few days, time is usually enough.

This is genuinely a beginner job. No plumbing is disconnected and no tools beyond scissors or a bread knife are needed.

Buy the right size

Foam tube is sold by the bore of the pipe it fits. UK domestic pipework is mostly 15mm (most radiator and tap runs) and 22mm (primary boiler and cylinder pipework, bath feeds), with some 28mm near larger boilers. Measure the outside of your pipe or take a photo with a tape measure in shot. Then choose the wall thickness: 13mm wall foam is the cheap standard, but for lofts, garages and anywhere unheated, pay slightly more for 25mm wall. The British Standard for frost protection in cold lofts effectively calls for thick-walled insulation, and the price difference is pennies per metre.

Fit it properly

  • Push the slit tube over the pipe and butt each length tightly against the next. Gaps are where pipes freeze.
  • Tape every joint and the slit itself with the manufacturer's tape or strong cloth tape. Foam that springs open is doing half its job.
  • Cut 45 degree mitres at elbows and tees, like a picture frame, so corners are covered. This is the step most people skip and it matters, because fittings freeze first.
  • Do not leave bare valves, pumps or stopcocks in cold spaces. Wrap them in felt or foil lagging, or buy moulded valve jackets.
  • Outdoors, use UV-stable rubber lagging or protect foam with a waterproof wrap, because ordinary grey foam degrades in sunlight and soaks up rain.

For a pipe run you genuinely cannot keep above freezing, such as a supply to a detached garage, electric trace heating cable under the lagging is the belt-and-braces answer. It uses a few watts per metre, thermostatically switched, and only runs in frost conditions.

sections.h2: Keeping pipes safe in a cold snap
sections.html:
Lagging is the permanent measure. When a hard frost is forecast, a few habits close the remaining gap:

  • Keep some heat on. In prolonged freezing weather, heating the house to around 13 to 15°C overnight, or using a frost setting if your thermostat has one, costs far less than a burst. If you are away in winter, leave the heating on a low setting rather than off, or drain the system for long absences.
  • Open the loft hatch during severe cold so warm house air can reach loft pipework and tanks. It feels wasteful, and it is, slightly, but only for a few nights a year.
  • Find your stopcock now. Usually under the kitchen sink or where the mains enters the house. Check it actually turns. In a burst, minutes matter.
  • Drip a vulnerable tap. If one pipe run has frozen before, leaving the tap it feeds very slightly open during the worst nights keeps water moving and relieves pressure.
  • Deal with draughts at pipe entry points. Cold air blowing through the gap where a pipe passes through a wall can freeze that one spot even in an otherwise mild void. Sealing these gaps is standard draught-proofing and protects the pipe at the same time.

If a pipe does freeze: turn off the stopcock, open the affected tap, and warm the frozen section gently with a hairdryer, hot water bottles or towels soaked in warm water, working back from the tap. Never use a blowtorch or heat gun, which can damage joints, melt plastic pipe and start fires. If the pipe has already split, leave the stopcock off and call a plumber. A frozen external condensate pipe is gentler work: pour warm (not boiling) water over it, then reset the boiler.

These checks belong on your winter energy checklist, ideally done in October before the first frost rather than during it.

Pipe lagging is a £15 to £30 job for most houses, it needs no skills beyond cutting foam with a knife, and on gas heating it typically repays its cost within the first winter through reduced heat loss alone. The real return is the burst you never have: with average frozen pipe claims above £17,000, almost nothing else in home maintenance offers this much protection per pound spent.

Prioritise unheated spaces, the pipework around the boiler and cylinder, the boiler condensate pipe and outside taps. Use 25mm wall foam in lofts and garages, tape every joint, mitre the corners, and pair the job with the rest of your insulation work, since a freshly insulated loft floor makes the loft colder and the pipes up there more vulnerable, not less.

faq.q: How much does pipe lagging save per year?
faq.a: Roughly £3 per metre per year on gas for hot pipework running through cold spaces, and around £9 per metre on electric heating, at July to September 2026 cap rates. A typical house with 5 to 10 metres of exposed hot pipe saves £15 to £30 a year, so the materials pay for themselves within the first winter. The avoided risk of a burst pipe is worth far more than the energy saving.

faq.q: What thickness of pipe lagging do I need?
faq.a: Standard 13mm wall foam is fine for hot pipes inside the heated part of the house. For lofts, garages, underfloor voids and anywhere unheated, use 25mm wall foam or thicker, which is what British Standard frost protection guidance effectively requires for cold lofts. Match the bore to your pipe: most UK domestic pipework is 15mm or 22mm.

faq.q: At what temperature do pipes freeze in a house?
faq.a: Water freezes at 0°C, but pipes inside a house usually only freeze when the space around them stays below freezing for several hours, which is why lofts, garages and pipe runs in draughty voids fail first. Keeping the house at around 13 to 15°C in severe cold, opening the loft hatch and lagging exposed runs protects almost all of them through a typical British cold snap.

faq.q: How do I thaw a frozen pipe safely?
faq.a: Turn off the water at the stopcock, open the tap fed by the frozen pipe, then warm the pipe gently with a hairdryer, hot water bottles or warm wet towels, working back from the tap towards the blockage. Never use a blowtorch or heat gun. If the pipe has split, leave the water off and call a plumber. For a frozen boiler condensate pipe outside, pour warm water over it and reset the boiler.

faq.q: Should cold water pipes be lagged too?
faq.a: Yes, in any unheated space. Lagging a cold pipe saves no energy, but it slows how fast the pipe loses heat to freezing air, which is usually enough to get through a few days of frost. Cold pipes in lofts, garages and under suspended floors are the most common burst points, so they should be lagged with thick-walled foam even though they will never repay anything on the bill.
